text: MAQ in procedure in prone position was carried out for a lower esophageal tumor, a squamous cell carcinoma in a 60-year-old woman with a past medical history significant for COPD with a BMI of 14.
At postoperative day seven, she developed chylothorax.
Chylothorax, after a zoophagectomy, remains a challenging clinical problem with a mortality rate reaching 30 to 50% if left untreated.
Among other identified causes, thoracic duct injury include anatomical variants of the thoracic duct and a low BMI.
Its treatment remains controversial and is based on conservative radiological and surgical techniques.

After failure of the initial conservative management with total parenteral nutrition and MPO due to the large callus output, a thoracoscopic ligation of the thoracic duct was performed at postoperative day 13.
During thoracoscopy, an ischemic anastomotic leak was incidentally found and sutured.
A periparative endoscopy was then carried out to evaluate the viability of the gastric tube.
At endoscopy, we were able to identify a 3 cm perianastomotic ischemic area.
A fully covered, self-expandable metal stent was used to protect the anastomosis.
The stents were secured to the esophageal mucosa to prevent migration with an oridoscope padlock clip from Aponos Medical.
A radiological embolization of the thoracic duct was attempted with success.

This also demonstrated an anatomical variation of the thoracic duct course and of its tributaries, which could explain both the injury during minimal invasive esophagectomy and the unsuccessful attempt to surgically ligate the duct.
The self-expandable metal stent was changed at six weeks and finally removed 12 weeks after the operation with complete healing of the leak and normal oesophageal mucosa.
Postoperative anastomotic leaks and chylothorax are associated with significant postoperative mortality and morbidity.
There is no generally accepted consensus directing management.
Here we show how the combination of different minimally invasive modalities may be successfully used to manage complex cases such as this.
